sections.title: Present Tense Modifier: -는
sections.explanation: In Korean, relative clauses come BEFORE the noun they modify (the opposite of English). To describe a noun with a present-tense action, you attach -는 to the action verb stem. This works like 'who is doing' or 'that is doing' in English. For example: 먹다 (to eat) → 먹는 사람 (a person who is eating). 읽다 (to read) → 읽는 책 (a book that [someone] is reading). The modifier -는 is used for action verbs in the present tense. For descriptive verbs (adjectives) in the present, you use -(으)ㄴ instead, not -는. For example: 크다 (to be big) → 큰 집 (a big house), not 크는 집. 작다 (to be small) → 작은 가방 (a small bag). The 있다/없다 verbs are exceptions — they use -는: 재미있는 영화 (an interesting movie).
sections.tip: Action verbs in present tense → -는. Descriptive verbs (adjectives) in present tense → -(으)ㄴ. This is the most important distinction for noun modification. Remember: 있다 and 없다 behave like action verbs here, using -는.

examples.en: The food I'm eating now is delicious
Let's meet in front of the big building (크다 → 큰)
I watched an interesting movie (있다 uses -는)

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'a person who reads books'?
exercises.explanation: For present-tense action verbs, use -는. 읽다 → 읽는. So 'a person who reads books' = 책을 읽는 사람.

exercises.prompt: Which modifier form is correct for 예쁘다 (to be pretty) modifying a noun in present tense?
exercises.explanation: Descriptive verbs (adjectives) use -(으)ㄴ for present-tense noun modification, not -는. 예쁘다 → 예쁜. Example: 예쁜 꽃 (a pretty flower).

options: I have a friend who will live in Seoul
I have a friend who lives in Seoul
I have a friend who lived in Seoul
exercises.explanation: 사는 is the present modifier form of 살다 (to live): 살 + -는 → 사는 (ㄹ drops before ㄴ). So it means 'a friend who lives in Seoul.'

sections.title: Past Tense Modifier: -(으)ㄴ
sections.explanation: To modify a noun with a past-tense action (something that was done or has been completed), you use -(으)ㄴ with action verbs. Add -ㄴ if the stem ends in a vowel, -은 if it ends in a consonant. For example: 먹다 → 먹은 음식 (food that [someone] ate). 만들다 → 만든 케이크 (a cake that [someone] made — ㄹ drops before ㄴ). 가다 → 간 사람 (a person who went). Notice this is the same form -(으)ㄴ that descriptive verbs use for present tense — but for action verbs, it indicates past tense. Context and verb type make the meaning clear: 작은 집 (a small house — 작다 is descriptive, present) vs. 지은 집 (a house that was built — 짓다 is action, past).
sections.tip: The key to mastering Korean modifiers: -(으)ㄴ with descriptive verbs = present meaning, -(으)ㄴ with action verbs = past meaning. This is confusing at first but becomes intuitive with practice.

examples.en: The food I ate yesterday was delicious
It's a cake that I made
The person who already left didn't come back
Please read the letter I wrote

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'the book I read yesterday'?
exercises.explanation: For past-tense action verbs modifying a noun, use -(으)ㄴ. 읽다 → 읽은. 'The book I read yesterday' = 어제 읽은 책.
만든 is the past modifier of 만들다 (ㄹ drops before ㄴ): 만들 → 만든. So 엄마가 만든 음식 = food that mom made.

exercises.prompt: In '작은 집에서 살았어요,' is 작은 present or past?
options: Present — 작다 is a descriptive verb
Future — something that will be small
exercises.explanation: 작다 is a descriptive verb (adjective). For descriptive verbs, -(으)ㄴ indicates present tense: 작은 집 = a small house (that IS small). The past tense of the sentence comes from 살았어요 (lived).

sections.title: Future/Prospective Modifier: -(으)ㄹ
sections.explanation: To modify a noun with a future or prospective meaning — something that will happen, is expected to happen, or is intended — use -(으)ㄹ. Add -ㄹ if the stem ends in a vowel, -을 if it ends in a consonant. This works with both action and descriptive verbs. For example: 먹다 → 먹을 음식 (food to eat / food that will be eaten). 가다 → 갈 곳 (a place to go). 읽다 → 읽을 책 (a book to read). 마시다 → 마실 것 (something to drink). This modifier is essential in many fixed expressions: -(으)ㄹ 수 있다 (can do), -(으)ㄹ 때 (when), -(으)ㄹ 것이다 (will do), -(으)ㄹ 줄 알다 (know how to).
sections.tip: Think of -(으)ㄹ as 'to [verb]' or 'that will [verb].' It suggests something not yet realized. This is why it appears in so many grammatical patterns about possibility, intention, and the future.

examples.en: I decided on a place to go tomorrow
There are many books to read
Please give me some water to drink

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'something to do' using 하다?
exercises.explanation: 하다 → stem 하 + ㄹ = 할. 'Something to do' or 'something that will be done' = 할 것.

options: I'm looking for a house I'm living in
I'm looking for a house I lived in
I'm looking for a house to live in
exercises.explanation: 살 is the future modifier of 살다 (to live): 살 + -(으)ㄹ → 살 (ㄹ stem, already ends in ㄹ). So 살 집 = a house to live in (prospective).

exercises.prompt: Which sentence uses the correct modifier tense? 'I want to see the movie that will be released next week.'
options: 다음 주에 개봉할 영화를 보고 싶어요
다음 주에 개봉하는 영화를 보고 싶어요
다음 주에 개봉한 영화를 보고 싶어요
exercises.explanation: Since the movie has not yet been released (it will be released next week), the future modifier -(으)ㄹ is most appropriate: 개봉할 영화 (movie that will be released). Though -는 is also sometimes used for scheduled future events.
